intro: Keep and Anteon both show up under the observability category, but they solve slightly different jobs. Keep uses a free model, while Anteon runs on subscription from $0.25 with free tier. Lock-in is low for Keep and medium for Anteon. Transparency lands at 5/5 versus 3/5. Keep fits teams working on aggregate and deduplicate alerts from 50+ tools, while Anteon is a closer match when the job is distributed systems debugging. Worth noting: Keep is explicitly not for teams needing battle-tested AIOps with dedicated 24/7 support; Anteon is explicitly not for non-technical projects. The honest trade-off: Keep trades off on early project, features still stabilizing; Anteon trades off on single-server apps with basic logging. On the plus side, Keep highlights open-source AIOps with 50+ tool integrations, while Anteon points to distributed systems debugging. Keep's documentation also calls out alert deduplication and correlation in one layer. Anteon similarly notes log aggregation and analysis.
quick_take: Keep is for aggregate and deduplicate alerts from 50+; Anteon is for distributed systems debugging; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Keep if your project is aggregate and deduplicate alerts from 50+ tools, you want to keep future migration cheap.
verdict_b: Choose Anteon if your project is distributed systems debugging, a subscription starting at $0.25 fits your budget, medium lock-in is an acceptable trade-off.

faq.a: Keep uses a free model, and Anteon uses subscription from $0.25 with free tier. The pricing models are different, so a direct cheaper-than comparison depends on your volume and usage pattern.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Keep to Anteon?
faq.a: Our data puts Keep at low lock-in, and Anteon at medium lock-in (k8s ebpf monitoring, open core). Moving from Keep to Anteon should be manageable, though you'll still need to replay integrations and re-test flows end-to-end.
Both score 4/5 on developer experience in our data, so there's no clear winner on that axis. Keep does edge ahead on pricing/docs transparency (5/5 vs 3/5), which can make evaluation faster.

faq.q: Is Anteon a good alternative to Keep?
faq.a: Anteon is a reasonable alternative to Keep when your workload leans more toward distributed systems debugging than platform teams wanting open alternative to PagerDuty AIOps or BigPanda. The pricing model shifts too — Keep is a free model, Anteon is subscription from $0.25 with free tier — so expect the cost profile to change as well. One caveat: Anteon is explicitly not for non-technical projects, so check that constraint against your use-case before switching.
